data.title: Tilray Has Already Done The Hard Part
data.description:
Introduction

Even a cursory glance at the industry will reveal that cannabis stocks have been taking a beating over the last few years. Legalization, once hailed as the ‘Green Rush,’ and fierce competition have eaten into margins and profitability. However, there are a few bright spots on the horizon, mainly linked to a renewed push towards federal reclassification in the USA. There are a few reasons to be bullish on Tilray (TLRY) if this does indeed occur. The company is positioned to benefit disproportionately if U.S. legalization arrives while it remains solvent, diversified, and operationally ready.

Tilray’s Business is Increasingly International

The company is increasingly pivoting towards international distribution. Although Canadian cannabis remained a large contributor to revenue last year, the international segment grew significantly. For FY 2025, the company posted revenue growth of 19% in this segment, having redirected some of its domestic stock towards export.

This trend has continued into fiscal 2026. For Q2 2026, the company reported a 36% increase in international cannabis sales versus a 6% increase in Canadian adult-use revenue. Although the company beat on revenue with a record quarter, margins paint a more nuanced picture. Both gross profit and gross margin contracted, but thanks to strong volume, the overall net loss narrowed from $85.3 million to $41.8 million year over year.

Europe as a Proving Ground

Tilray’s European operations contribute most of its growth in the international segment, with Germany leading the way following its legalization in 2024. Its distribution platform in Germany serves some 13,000 pharmacies through Tilray Pharma. From its European hub in Portugal, Tilray exports to some 20 countries.

With the launch of Tilray Medical Italia, the company is consolidating and rebranding its Italian operations while expanding its portfolio of EU-GMP certified medical cannabis products approved by the Italian Ministry of Health. Distribution is supported by a partnership with Molteni Farmaceutici, providing access to hospitals and pharmacies nationwide. The move reinforces Tilray’s strategy of scaling international medical cannabis through regulated channels, emphasizing margin quality, physician access, and repeat prescription demand rather than volume-led recreational growth, as reported by Cannabis Business Times.

Aside from contributing significantly to revenue, Tilray’s success in Europe has taught it how to navigate a tightly regulated industry in developed foreign markets. Although Europe can’t be compared to the United States due to its fragmentation, vast cultural and linguistic differences, and uneven moves towards legalization, it has proven to be a fertile proving ground.

The Road to Washington

By far the biggest news story in the cannabis industry over the past year is the plan to reschedule cannabis in the United States at a federal level. This would move cannabis from a Schedule I to a Schedule III, essentially reclassifying it as a less dangerous drug. Although this does not constitute federal legalization, it does reduce a number of important hurdles for cannabis companies, including tax and research restrictions. Importantly, this move would not open up the USA as an export market for Tilray. Instead, it signals increasing political momentum towards legalization.

Fortunately for Tilray, it already has a way into the USA through its beverage operations. Tilray Beverages is the fourth largest producer of craft beer in the United States. This provides the company with national distribution relationships, retail access, logistics, and consumer branding infrastructure that it could quickly put to use once legislation allows for things like cannabis-infused beverages.

What It Would Take for Tilray to Be Profitable

Tilray’s path to profitability does not require a dramatic recovery in Canadian cannabis pricing or a sudden explosion in U.S. demand. First, international medical cannabis needs to continue growing at a double-digit pace. Recent results suggest this is already happening, driven primarily by Germany and other European markets where medical access is expanding under regulated frameworks. These markets tend to offer more stable pricing and higher margin potential than Canadian adult-use cannabis.

Secondly, Tilray is no longer struggling to survive. Stabilization in the domestic market means that its core business is intact. It now has some breathing room to grow on its own terms. For Tilray, this means aggressively targeting foreign markets and establishing both infrastructural and regulatory routes. Its success in Europe means it already knows how to do this.

What completes the profitability bridge is what happens below EBITDA. For Tilray Brands, the primary drag has not been gross margin collapse but interest expense and restructuring runoff following several years of consolidation. As those costs normalize, incremental EBITDA has a clearer path to converting into cash flow. The key inflection is not rapid growth but EBITDA consistently covering interest and maintenance capital expenditure, which reduces dilution pressure and balance-sheet risk.

Valuation Shows Skepticism, Not Collapse

On valuation, Tilray looks inexpensive relative to what it already produces rather than what it might become. At roughly 1.2x EV to sales and around 0.6x price to book, the market is valuing the company as if its asset base and revenue mix have limited long-term utility. That is a harsh assumption for a business that has already absorbed multiple rounds of write-downs, moved back to a net cash position, and now generates positive adjusted EBITDA. This is not a deep value stock on earnings, but it is inexpensive on assets and revenue, particularly for a company that has diversified away from a single troubled market. If EBITDA stabilizes and book value proves durable, valuation support does not require heroic assumptions.

Conclusion

Tilray is increasingly a diversified platform with international medical exposure, U.S. consumer infrastructure, and a balance sheet designed to survive prolonged regulatory delay. The company’s path to profitability runs less through near-term cannabis pricing and more through Washington’s slow but consequential movement toward reform. Rescheduling alone does not unlock the U.S. market for Tilray, but it reinforces the strategic logic behind the company’s positioning. If federal legalization or a regulated medical framework ultimately follows, Tilray is positioned to participate without having to rebuild its operating model. If reform takes longer, the company’s diversification reduces the risk of permanent impairment.

data.title: 3 U.S. Tech Stocks To Watch After Nasdaq 100 Bullish Breakout Above 25,830 (Technical Analysis)
data.description:
By Kelvin Wong

The price actions of the Nasdaq 100 CFD index (a proxy of the Nasdaq 100 E-mini futures) have staged the expected bullish breakout above the former minor 7-week range resistance of 25,830 and rallied by almost 1% on Tuesday, and it has continued to extend its gains on Wednesday, January 28 Asian session with a rally of 0.6% to record an intraday level of 26,170 at the time of writing.

The year-to-date laggard among the major US stock indices has started to catch up. So far, the week-to-date performance as of 26 January 2026, the cash market Nasdaq 100 has recorded a gain of 1.3%, surpassing the S&P 500 (+0.9%), Dow Jones Industrial Average (-0.2%), and the small-cap Russell 2000 (-0.1%).

Amazon, Cisco Systems, Meta Platforms may outperform Nasdaq 100

This report will highlight three US technology stocks (Amazon (AMZN), Cisco Systems (CSCO) and Meta Platforms (META)) that may outperform the Nasdaq 100 (in terms of positive alpha generation) filter by technical factors and volatility-adjusted relative strength (VARS).

In a quick note, VARS is an indicator that normalises momentum by accounting for a stock's volatility, helping traders identify genuine market strength and avoid misleading signals from high-beta, high-volatility shares.

Read more about this guide on volatility-adjusted relative strength: VARS: How to prevent the high-beta trap and find the relevant stocks

Here are the technical charts of the three US technology stocks that may generate positive alpha over the medium-term horizon (1 to 3 weeks).

Amazon (AMZN) bullish reintegration back above 20-day & 50-day MA

Fig. 1: Amazon medium-term trend as of Jan 17, 2026 (Source: TradingView)

Amazon will report its Q4 2025 earnings on Thursday, February 5, after the close of the US session.

In the past three trading sessions, the share price of Amazon has rebounded and traded back above its 20-day and 50-day moving averages.

The volatility-adjusted relative strength of Amazon against the Nasdaq 100 exchange-traded fund has started to slope upwards significantly since December 3, 2025, and traded above its 50-day moving average as well as above its zero line since December 26, 2025 (see Fig. 1).

This observation on VARS suggests that Amazon has started to outperform the Nasdaq 100, a transition from its multi-month underperformance that took place from April 15, 2025 to October 17, 2025.

In addition, its daily RSI momentum indicator is also evolving in a bullish momentum condition as it managed to stage a rebound from its ascending support and crossed above the 50 level, while still below its overbought region (above the 70 level).

Watch the 226.50 key medium-term pivotal support to maintain the bullish bias for the next medium-term resistances to come in at 258.60 (current all-time high) and 275.26 next in the first step.

However, failure to hold at 226.50 and a daily close below it will jeopardize the bulls to trigger a multi-week corrective decline to expose the next medium-term supports at 211.40 and 194.70 next.

Cisco Systems (CSCO) bullish momentum breakout

Fig. 2: Cisco Systems medium-term trend as of Jan 27, 2026 (Source: TradingView)

Cisco Systems will report its Q4 2025 earnings on Wednesday, February 18, 2026, after the close of the US session.

The share price of Cisco has traded back above its 20-day and 50-day moving averages.

The daily RSI momentum indicator has just staged a bullish breakout from its prior descending resistance and rebounded back above the 50 level on January 26, 2026.

In addition, the volatility-adjusted relative strength of Cisco against the Nasdaq 100 exchange-traded fund has started to slope upwards significantly since October 29, 2025 and traded above its 50-day moving average as well as above its zero line since December 29, 2035.

This observation on VARS suggests that Cisco has started to outperform the Nasdaq 100, a transition from its multi-month underperformance that took place from May 27, 2025 to September 22, 2025.

Watch the 72.80 key medium-term pivotal support to maintain the bullish bias for the next medium-term resistances to come in at 82.00 (current all-time high), 85.62, and 90.79/91.54 next in the first step (see Fig. 2).

On the other hand, failure to hold at 72.80 and a daily close below invalidates the bullish scenario to open scope for a multi-week corrective decline to expose the next medium-term supports at 68.89 and 66.50 next.

Meta Platforms (META)'s prior 27% plunge halted at long-term ascending channel support

Fig. 3: Meta Platforms medium-term trend as of Jan 27, 2026 (Source: TradingView)

Meta Platforms will report its Q4 2025 earnings on Wednesday, January 28, 2026, after the close of the US session.

The 27% decline seen in Meta Platforms from its current all-time high of 796.25 printed on August 15, 2025 to the November 19, 2025 low of 581.25 has managed to pause right at the long-term secular ascending channel support in place since the October 2022 low.

Several positive technical elements have emerged. The price actions of Meta Platforms have formed a “higher low” on January 20, 2025, rallied by around 9.8%, and closed above its 20-day and 50-day moving averages (see Fig. 3).

The daily RSI momentum indicator has staged a bullish breakout above its descending resistance and the 50 level, which suggests medium-term bullish momentum may have resurfaced.

The volatility-adjusted relative strength (VARS) of Meta Platforms against the S&P 500 exchange-traded fund (SPY) has started to turn up (shaped a “higher low”) above zero and traded back up above its 50-day moving average. These observations suggest the relative weakness of Meta Platforms may be starting to gain back some strength.

Watch the 585.77 key medium-term pivotal support, and a clearance above 705.20 intermediate resistance may see the medium-term resistances coming in at 758.40 and 793.70 (current all-time high area).

On the flip side, a break and a daily close below 585.77 invalidates the bullish recovery scenario to expose the next medium-term supports at 516.50 and 479.80.

data.title: EDD: The Rise Of Emerging Market Debt
data.description:
The Case for Emerging Market Debt

Emerging market debt has entered a new bull cycle following previous years of extremely poor performance. While sovereign defaults were a major concern in previous years, many emerging markets have substantially improved their economies, and the likelihood of defaults in 2026 is very low.

Domestic debt vehicles like the Morgan Stanley Emerging Markets Domestic Debt Fund (EDD) are positioned as unique diversifiers in the market and will likely deliver moderate returns in 2026 following the stellar rally of EMs in 2025.

While most emerging market ETFs focus on larger MSCI EM constituents, EDD invests in smaller emerging markets like Egypt and Kenya, which are very difficult for US investors to access. Consequently, EDD is a must for investors who want to have diversified exposure to emerging markets in 2026. This ETF still offers a very attractive yield, which has historically been very consistent.

Both EM equities and debt have rallied substantially in 2025, and returns in 2026-2027 will likely be much more limited. For this reason, EDD looks like a solid hold, as it offers diversification benefits but has more limited upside in the upcoming years.

Morgan Stanley Emerging Market Domestic Debt Fund

The Morgan Stanley Emerging Market Domestic Debt Fund focuses on investing in a variety of local currency-denominated debt, primarily sovereign debt. However, this fund also invests in corporate debt and quasi-sovereign debt.

EDD only invests around 9% of its assets in corporate bonds while primarily focusing on sovereign debt in many smaller emerging markets.

One of the main appeals of this ETF is that it adequately covers smaller emerging markets that many emerging market equity funds do not have exposure to. This makes EDD a unique vehicle, as markets like India and China dominate the EM sovereign debt markets.

Overall, it is best for investors who are searching for higher yields in emerging markets, as major EM indices currently only yield around 2.2%. This ETF currently yields over 10% and has been very consistent in its distributions. This yield is appropriate given the larger country risk and higher management fee of this ETF.

Emerging Market Outlook

Emerging markets have successfully navigated 2025 trade war risks, delivering strong economic growth and stellar market returns. Growth will likely remain around 4% in emerging markets in 2026, while inflation will drop to around 5%.

Both emerging market equity and debt investments have performed very well during the past twelve months as many investors have been searching for alternatives to the higher-valued and lower-yielding S&P 500.

Emerging markets have come into focus as a solid alternative asset class because of the superior growth relative to developed markets. EMs will likely be able to sustain 4% growth, while many smaller emerging markets in regions like Africa will likely have stronger growth.

These markets have also tackled inflationary risks due to the superior response of Central Banks in emerging markets, which quickly hiked rates to combat inflation. Even basket cases like Nigeria, Argentina, and Turkey have had a strong decline in inflation.

Emerging Market Debt Performance and Outlook

While the majority of new debt issuance will take place in China and India, there is still ample value available in smaller emerging markets, many of which EDD targets in its investments. These emerging markets stand out for their comparatively higher yields, which have translated to stronger returns following the recent stability of emerging market currencies.

Although sentiment has been shaky in emerging markets in the past twelve months, many markets have delivered to the upside, and the macro outlook is still relatively favorable. Fitch Ratings currently has a net neutral rating for emerging market debt in 2026.

Overall, there have been many upgrades in emerging market credit ratings in the past couple of years, and sentiment will likely remain stable in 2026-2027. While 2022 was easily the superior entry point for EM debt, 2026 still looks like a solid time to accumulate high-yielding emerging market sovereign debt.

Emerging market Central Banks have shown their resilience post-2022, when they responded by aggressively hiking rates to combat inflation. Moreover, economies have remained resilient since early 2025 amid trade war tensions, shocking the market with strong 2025 gains.

Concerns over sovereign defaults have also abated in recent years, following massive defaults in 2022-2023. Most of the defaults were driven by the post-COVID economic concerns, and EM defaults have remained limited since 2023.

The recent weakness in the USD has also been a massive boon for domestic debt funds, resulting in a strong uptick in real returns.

High-yield emerging markets in particular have also seen a massive improvement in their credit quality ratings. Cases like Nigeria, Argentina, and other risky emerging markets have followed through with successful monetary policies that have eased inflation and supported the economy.

2025 yields for domestic debt funds were in the teens, while default rates will likely stay below 1% this year, making these funds an attractive pick this year. Historically, emerging market bonds have delivered solid returns, and 2026-2027 returns will likely fall in line with their more historical average return of 6.2% per annum over the past two decades.

Risks and Final Thoughts

The Morgan Stanley Emerging Market Domestic Debt Fund is a great vehicle to diversify one's portfolio, but it still carries a lot of risks in 2026-2027.

Elevated political risks in key emerging markets, including the recent tensions between China and Taiwan, could have a spillover impact on emerging market sentiment, although this ETF is still strongly geographically diversified and less exposed to larger EMs.

The performance of EM currencies has been a major driver of emerging market debt returns, so any currency volatility in 2026, following previous favorable years, could result in lower emerging market debt returns.

2026 is also not the best entry point for this ETF, as 2025 was an out-of-the-ordinary year for EDD, while returns were more or less flat between 2021 and early 2025.

Many of the smaller EM constituents that this ETF invests in, such as Egypt and Kenya, have struggled with higher inflation and other issues relative to other emerging markets. These markets may be much more volatile during any emerging market pullbacks, as many larger EM constituents are in a much better position in terms of inflation and debt profiles.

The final, most significant threat to note is that sovereign emerging debt levels have hit record highs following Covid, and many markets could face distressed situations. While 2024-2025 has been favorable, and 2026 has a solid outlook, this is a huge risk to continue monitoring in subsequent years, which could hit EM currencies and result in poor performance in future years.

Overall, the risk factors in 2026 appear acceptable, and the high yield of this ETF is favorable relative to the risks emerging markets face in the upcoming years. Now is not the best entry point, but still a suitable time to continue holding EDD, in line with one's strategy to diversify away from dominant emerging markets like India, China, and Taiwan.

data.title: Tilray: Rescheduling Rally Meets Equity Erosion & Short Interest Volatility
data.description:
I previously covered Tilray Brands (TLRY) in October 2022, discussing how the stock might continue to underperform in the intermediate term, attributed to the uncertain path to US federal cannabis legalization, the minimal profitability, and the elevated long-term debts.

Combined with the elevated SBC expenses and the potentially painful stock price correction from prior highs, I had rated the stock as a Hold (Neutral) then.

In this article, I shall discuss why I am reiterating my Hold (Neutral) rating for the TLRY stock here, despite the tailwinds from the growing international cannabis business and the upcoming US rescheduling, allowing them to leverage their expertise and their vertically integrated, global supply chain.

My caution is attributed to the likely to be lengthy rescheduling process, the intense competition from US-based MSOs with local expertise, the ongoing shareholder erosion, and the potential volatility from the elevated short interest ratio.

TLRY May Benefit From US Cannabis Rescheduling

TLRY 1Y Stock Price

Since my last coverage, the cannabis sector has been extremely volatile indeed, with TLRY losing much of its value since the pandemic highs of $214s while entering penny stock levels of $3.60s by June 2025, before charting an outsized rally to retest the prior resistance levels of $13s in December 2025.

Much of its recent recovery is attributed to the US President Donald Trump signing "an executive order to classify marijuana as a less dangerous drug" on December 18, 2025, with the rescheduling from Schedule I to Schedule III of the Controlled Substances Act "marking a turning point in the U.S. cannabis space."

1. Leveraging Cannabis Expertise

Part of the exuberance is warranted indeed, attributed to TLRY's position as "the #1 cannabis producer in Canada by revenue, the fourth largest craft beer brewer in the United States and a market leader in branded hemp wellness products across North America, where our high-protein hemp food portfolio holds a nearly 60% market share."

Therefore, while the company has yet to sell any cannabis products within the US, attributed to the Nasdaq ruling where companies "cannot initially list or continue the listing of a company whose current or planned activities are in violation of U.S. federal law or the law in a jurisdiction where the company operates," things may soon change upon the eventual cannabis rescheduling.

This is a material development indeed, since the US cannabis market is expected to grow from $16.85B in 2025 to $65.26B by 2035, expanding at an accelerated CAGR of +14.5%.

It is unsurprising then, that TLRY has rapidly formed "Tilray Medical USA, Inc., further strengthening its commitment to leading medical cannabis innovation and growth" in December 2025, allowing them to "advance its U.S. medical cannabis operations following federal cannabis rescheduling and related regulatory developments."

This is while hinting at its future engagements to drive future medical monetization opportunities:

And again, engage with the outreach of the government, with the FDA and with our -- working with hospitals, working with research, doing clinical studies. And that's what we're looking to do there in regards to our U.S. entry into Tilray U.S....

And we have so much research in pain, anxiety, cancer-related drugs, cancer anti-vomiting drugs, PTSD and taking that science and taking those -- and taking our genetics and strains and working with hospitals and potentially strategically aligning with a pharma company to execute that within the U.S. is what we're looking to do.

(Seeking Alpha)

TLRY's focus on medical opportunities are not surprising indeed, since the US President explicitly highlights the focus on "medical marijuana and cannabidiol research" in the Executive Order, with it implying the federal rescheduling prospects only for medical uses and the recreational uses potentially being left out.

This conjecture is given further credibility through the administrator of the Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services, Dr. Mehmet Oz's announcement:

The innovation center models are going to allow millions of Americans on Medicare to become eligible to receive CBD as early as April of next year and at no charge, if their doctors recommend them. (CNN)

This implies TLRY's potential beneficiary status beyond its current Canadian Medical Cannabis revenues of $12.38M in H1'26 (-4.2% YoY) and the overall Cannabis Revenues of $132.04M (+4% YoY), allowing them to leverage their existing expertise across production, distribution, manufacturing, and advisory services for pharmaceutical-grade medical cannabis products.

2. Complementary Benefits

There may be a complementary impact on TLRY's beverage operations as well, with it presenting opportunities for CBD or THC infused drinks.

For reference, CBD stands for cannabidiol derived from the cannabis plant, albeit without psychoactive effects (feeling high) and THC stands for delta-9-tetrahydrocannabinol, also derived from the plant and with psychoactive effects, with the ingredients often infused in many products, including oils, edibles, topicals, and vapes, amongst others.

For now, TLRY already launched numerous hemp-derived products and Delta-9 (THC infused) beverages as a way to tap into the "new and alternative adult beverages" segment, reported under the Wellness segment with revenues of $29.82M in H1'26 (+1.6% YoY).

Its prospects are relatively bright as well, based on the projected sales growth for hemp-derived THC drinks from $382M in 2024 to $750M in 2029, expanding at a 5Y CAGR of +14.4%, with it further aided by the "50% share in Canada," allowing them to leverage its existing beverage (alcoholic and non alcoholic) supply chain and distribution partnership upon federal rescheduling.

Naturally, this is assuming a smooth rescheduling process - an unlikely development given the opposition and numerous regulatory hurdles, including the prior hemp-derived THC ban, as observed in the recent Executive Order including a request "to update the statutory definition of final hemp-derived cannabinoid products to allow Americans to benefit from access to appropriate full-spectrum CBD products."

Given the intermediate term uncertainty, I urge readers to monitor the rescheduling development closely.

3. TLRY's Mixed Performance Metrics

For now, TLRY has been able to emerge relatively stronger from the prior COVID boom and bust environment, through the robust H1'26 overall revenues of $427M (+4.1% YoY) albeit with deteriorating adj EBITDA margins of 4.3% (-0.1 points YoY), with it building upon the FY2025 performance at +4.1% YoY and 6.6% (-1 points YoY).

This is on top of the management's reaffirmed FY2026 adj EBITDA guidance of $67M at the midpoint (+21.8% YoY), with it underscoring their ability to remain resilient despite the challenging environment.

Much of TLRY's tailwinds are attributed to its success in the higher margin international market with +30% YoY expansion in FQ2'26, "particularly Germany, the U.K. and Poland," accelerated beyond Canada's growth profile by +4.4% YoY, thanks to their ability to capitalize on the expanding global adoption of cannabis and/or CBD/THC infused offerings for medical and/or recreational use.

Combined with the net cash position on its balance sheet and the international/US cannabis tailwinds, it is unsurprising that the consensus expects the company to report single digits top-line growth moving forward, as TLRY emerges as one of the few winners of the cannabis race, after the numerous boom and bust stories over the past few years.

TLRY Remains A Hold Here

Despite completing a 1-for-10 Reverse Stock Split in December 2025, it is undeniable that TLRY remains inherently volatile at current levels, as similarly observed in the elevated short interest ratio of 14.1% by the time of writing.

The lack of bullish support is also observed in TLRY's moderating technical indicators after the outsized rally since the June 2025 bottom, with sentiments seemingly mixed after the recent FQ2'26 earnings call in early January 2026.

TLRY Valuations

Thanks to the recent volatility and the deteriorating profit margins, it is unsurprising that TLRY boasts a mixed valuation across the cheap FWD EV/Sales of 1.21x and the expensive FWD EV/EBITDA of 17.29x, down from to the 4Y mean of 3.80x/38.28x albeit still expensive compared to the sector median of 3.84x/12.91x, respectively.

A similar conclusion may be observed after comparing to its US-based Multi-State Operators [MSO]/cannabis peers as well, including:

  • Cresco Labs (CRLBF) at FWD EV/Sales valuations of 1.38x and FWD EV/EBITDA valuations of 5.94x,
  • TerrAscend (TSNDF) at 1.74x/7.02x,
  • Green Thumb Industries (GTBIF) at 1.87x/6.64x,
  • Curaleaf Holdings (CURLF) at 2.44x/11.65x,
  • Aurora Cannabis (ACB) at 1.02x/6.55x, and
  • Trulieve Cannabis (TCNNF) at 1.53x/4.27x, respectively,

with it apparent that TLRY boasts a mixed return prospect at current levels, after the outsized stock price rally by +157.8% since the 52 week lows and the FOMO exuberance observed in the sector.

If anything, TLRY's upcoming entry into the US market remains uncertain, attributed to the prior MedMen bet seemingly being a net negative as the latter filed for bankruptcy in 2024 and putting the former's prior investments worth ~$166M to naught, pending further clarity on the ongoing liquidation process.

I am of the opinion that the company is likely to face great hurdles as well, attributed to the likely to be lengthy rescheduling process, the intense competition from US-based MSOs with local expertise, and the potential entry by legacy pharmaceutical players with deeper pockets.

Combined with the ongoing shareholder erosion to 110.34M in FQ2'26 (+27.5% YoY), the aggressive acquisition trends for beer portfolios, and the mixed synergies from the prior cannabis acquisitions/merger across Hexo and Aphria, I am of the opinion that it may be better to observe its execution for a little longer - resulting in my reiterated Hold rating for the TLRY stock here.

Do not chase this dip.

data.title: Intel: Same As Last Year, The Patient Investor Will Get Rewarded
data.description:
Q4 and Full-Year 2025 Earnings Takeaway

For Q4 2025, Intel Corporation (INTC) reported a revenue of $13.7 billion and an EPS of $0.15. For the full year, Intel generated revenue of $52.85 billion, slightly down from last year's $53.1 billion. This slight decline is due to supply constraint experienced in Q4 2025, which also led to the softer-than-expected guidance, resulting in the company's stock price declining by almost 14% despite beating analyst expectations.

Supply Constraint

During the earnings call, Intel management attributed the soft Q1 2026 guidance to the supply constraint, which is expected to trough in Q1 2026 and improve as the year progresses. Now, an investor may wonder why Intel is experiencing a supply constraint despite owning its manufacturing facilities. After all, if there is a constraint, it should be the companies fighting for manufacturing capacity that should be getting hit hard, and not the ones that own the manufacturing facilities. What should be happening right now is Intel out-supplying its competitors and gaining market share. Rightfully so, one of the analysts questioned the Intel management on why this is happening despite owning its fabs.

I do. I mean just to push on that a little bit. I mean, you guys have your own factories, like why are you in the inventory situation that you're in? And then -- I mean, even if you look at -- I get the whole idea about finished goods versus other stuff. But I mean like you have $11.6 billion of inventory. And yet, it's not in the right place at the right time. Like how does that happen?

Intel CFO David Zinser answered that this is what the market signaled to them 6 months prior to this surging demand.

That's largely a win. I'll tell you, Stacy. I think the biggest thing is that we if you go back 6 months or so ago and looked at what the outlook was. Core count was absolutely looking like it would increase, but the units were not expected to increase. And every hyperscaler customer we talked to was signaling that. And obviously, it has rapidly increased over the third and fourth quarter. And in talking to a few of them right before this call, I got the feeling like it was going to be a story we'd feel for several years.

Now, there is room for frustration among analysts and investors that Intel allowed itself to miss this chance to opportunistically take advantage of this surging demand after years of market share and revenue decline. However, one must also consider the fact that Intel got significant flak from analysts and investors for mismanaging its inventory during the PC glut 2 years ago. This is just an unfortunate dilemma that the company has to deal with. If the company decided to make a contrarian bet that demand will be better contrary to market signals, and they turned out to be wrong, then the market will nevertheless punish the stock. Furthermore, Intel's Q4 2025 inventory confirms the management's comment as evident by the decline in its 'Finished goods.'

The common rule to follow here is that increasing "Raw materials" or "Work in process" often indicates that there is an increasing demand for a company's product, while increasing "Finished goods" points to the contrary.

Regardless, I believe this issue is one that can be easily fixed due to the still massive manufacturing scale that Intel has, especially in the CPU market. While it seems eternal for short-term traders and investors, 1 quarter of sorting these kinks out is one that a long-term investor should be able to easily deal with. Intel is still in a unique position where it doesn't have to fight hard for manufacturing capacity that fabless companies such as Advanced Micro Devices (AMD), Apple (AAPL), Qualcomm (QCOM), and Nvidia (NVDA) do with TSMC (TSM).

Intel Client Computing Group

For Q4 2025, Intel Client Computing Group (CCG) delivered a revenue of $8.2 billion, down by almost 7%.

The revenue decline is attributed to the supply constraint mentioned earlier. The company was forced to shift production capacity away from its client group to the data center, which I believe is a prudent choice as the datacenter group is a more important segment due to its higher growth potential.

Meanwhile, operating profit for the quarter is around $2.2 billion, down by almost 31%, due to the unfavorable product mix driven by Lunar Lake, which continues to face margin pressure due to memory exposure.

I expect CCG's margin to improve as the year progresses, underpinned by the continued ramp of Panther Lake, given that it doesn't have a memory headwind to deal with. With Panther Lake, I am very optimistic that Intel will gain market share against AMD in the notebook segment due to its superior performance and efficiency. AMD's notebook product for this year is all but a refresh of its last year's Strix Point. There are still gaps that need to be filled in the high-end desktop segment. But I expect Intel's upcoming Nova Lake to stabilize and strengthen this gap. Despite a seemingly lukewarm performance by Intel's client segment, it was offset by the strong performance of Intel's Datacenter and AI Group (DCAI).

Intel Datacenter and AI Group

For the quarter, DCAI delivered a revenue of $4.7 billion, up by almost 7% YoY, and an operating profit of $1.3 billion, an increase of 225% YoY, driven by strong demand from traditional and AI hardware infrastructure. Intel management expects this strong performance to continue for the rest of the year.

Turning to non-financial development for DCAI, Intel CEO Lip Bu Tan announced that the company is merging its AI group with the datacenter segment due to the increasing importance of in-sync advancement and tight coordination of both CPU and GPU in AI. Another noteworthy development is that Intel is focusing its resources on developing the 16-memory channel "Diamond Rapids" and the acceleration of "Coral Rapids." I believe that this initiative is due to the lack of competitiveness of Diamond Rapids due to a lack of multithreading. Thus, it makes sense to allocate the company's precious resources to market segments where Diamond Rapids can win and to accelerate the development of Coral Rapids, which I believe will be more equipped to tackle the broader part of the server market due to the reintroduction of multithreading.

Another interesting growth vector for Intel DCAI is its ASIC segment, which is now at an annualized revenue run rate of around $1 billion. This is a remnant of the networking and edge group that was merged with CCG and DCAI. I believe that Intel made the right decision in not divesting this business unit as networking plays a crucial role in scaling AI infrastructure.

Intel Foundry

For Q4 2025, Intel Foundry delivered a revenue of $4.5 billion, with an operating loss of $2.5 billion.

Of the $4.5 billion revenue generated by the foundry, around $4.27 billion comes from intersegmental transactions, and the rest is from external clients, driven by the U.S. government projects and the deconsolidation of Altera.

Despite continuing losses, this quarter represents a significant milestone for Intel Foundry as it delivers the lead vehicle for the first Intel 18A product in the form of Panther Lake. This quarter also represents significant rumors, which I believe is one of the causes for its recent stock performance, which saw its stock price surge by almost 120% in a 6-month timeframe. These rumors range from Apple adopting Intel 18A-P, along with Nvidia and AMD, for Intel 14A. Intel's advanced packaging is also gaining significant interest across the industry from companies like Google (GOOG) and Broadcom (AVGO).

Again, despite the huge losses and the significant past mistakes made with this business, I believe that this is a very important growth vector for the company. The leading-edge semiconductor manufacturing is a very hard industry to enter and only 3 companies in the world have the capability to manufacture leading-edge wafer technology: TSMC, Intel and Samsung. While I don't like relying on the government as a catalyst in my investment decisions, Intel plays an important role in U.S. national security. I believe that Intel CEO Lip Bu Tan's broader understanding of the semiconductor industry will play a significant role in correcting the past mistakes of Intel Foundry. I like the significant emphasis Lip Bu Tan is communicating with investors, such as the need for a more service-oriented culture in the foundry, simpler process flow and design methodology, and a more decentralized structure by abandoning the decades-old "Copy Exact" manufacturing practice.

That said, I continue to believe Intel must eventually split its Product Team and Foundry to truly unlock the company's value. Designing and manufacturing a leading-edge semiconductor requires two distinct capabilities and competencies. No person or management in the world has the capability or mental fortitude to fully manage both well. I believe both require their own respective management and a board of directors, specialized in their respective disciplines, to truly unleash both groups' potential.

Valuation

Not much has changed since my last article about Intel regarding valuation. Despite the outstanding performance of Intel's stock price, which saw its stock price surging from $20 per share to around $54 per share, before plunging back to $45 per share, the company still sells at a reasonable valuation. But at this price, it no longer represents a deep value play where an investor is buying a company for its demonstrated performance on a discount, and buying the future for literally free. Intel used to sell at a price ($20 per share) where an investor was buying the demonstrated performance of the Intel Product alone at half the price, while getting the potential prosperity of Intel Foundry for free. Not to mention the multiple catalysts, which effectively mitigate the potential downside when the stock is selling at around $20 per share. I articulated this in the valuation section in my last year's article about Intel.

While I like that my investment in Intel has appreciated since then, the lack of aforementioned factors makes me cautious about further adding to my existing position at this current price level due to the increasing market expectation being priced into the stock, especially for a company with an insufficient track record. But doesn't Intel have a 20 to 30-year track as a CPU market leader? Yes, but the current semiconductor industry has drastically evolved that its previous track record no longer suffices. The past success Intel has enjoyed as a vertically integrated company is no longer equipped to compete in this more modularized industry structure, where companies with strong moats are the specialized ones. The company has dealt with this predicament by separating Intel Product and Intel Foundry. But I still believe that an eventual full split is a must.

That said, I believe Intel is still selling at a reasonable price. A price where total reliance on future performance is still not the main vindication of a stock purchase. For FY2025, Intel Products generated an operating profit of around $12.7 billion. This represents an enterprise value of around $190.5 billion using, I believe, a conservative 15x EV/EBIT multiple.

Why do I believe that it is conservative? I explain this in the valuation section of my last article: "Intel: Despite Strong Q3 2025, Recent Stock Price Rally Made The Stock Riskier."

At a conservative 15x multiple, Intel's enterprise value stood at around $190.5 billion, which means there is a $44 billion gap at its current enterprise value of $234 billion. This gap can be covered by the over $100 billion of invested capital Intel has deployed since announcing its intention to become a 3rd party foundry for external customers unless these assets become a sunk cost due to its inability to generate value. The recent rumors of multiple customer wins and industry interests indicate that Intel Foundry is executing well, suggesting that this will not be the case.

Now, I am also contemplating arbitrarily using a 20x EV/EBIT multiple as the recent performance of Intel's datacenter group, a more favorable outlook, and the performance leadership demonstrated by Panther Lake should warrant a higher multiple for the Product group. Thus, an investor buying at this price level is still buying Intel Products with a small discount and Foundry's prospects for free.

While the risk-to-reward ratio is no longer as attractive compared to last year, I believe the vindication I had last year about Intel still applies at this current price level. I can argue that at this current price, I'm still paying for the value of the Intel Product alone, while getting the foundry for free but at a substantially lower degree. I believe Intel Products' improving market leadership position and a foundry that is gaining momentum, will enable it to deliver significant shareholder value for the patient investor willing to hold for the long term.

Just like last year, a patient investor that hold with conviction will eventually get rewarded. The recent price action is all but a temporary setback and a good reality check that a stock price cannot diverge far away from its underlying value. I'd rather have a stock whose stock price appreciates in sync with its intrinsic value rather than one driven by short-term speculation. Therefore, I will maintain my Buy rating for Intel.

Risk To The Investment Thesis

Intel's outlook has significantly improved compared to last year in both its Foundry and Product businesses. However, it doesn't change the fact that the company's transformation is still ongoing and uncertain, especially on the Foundry side. The common adage in investing is that past performance can provide a rough guide to the future. This applies if past conditions will be somewhat similar to future conditions. Intel has no track record of manufacturing for external customers, unlike TSMC, which has been doing it since its inception. Therefore, we still have no assurance that Intel's vision with its foundry will materialize unless revenue starts flowing and market share gains start to commence.

While the uncertainty and doubt on the Intel Products have subsided, it still faces significant competition. Intel Products no longer has the undoubted market leadership it once had in the CPU market during its peak before its recent stumble.

data.title: TRX Gold Aims To Create Value For Its Shareholders
data.description:
TRX Gold Corporation's Bullish Outlook: What You Need to Know

If you're interested in gold mining and exploration companies benefiting from record gold prices, TRX Gold Corporation (TRX) should be on your radar. Although the company's gold production is still small, it is attracting investor attention, much like a large corporation. Value creation is developing promisingly, and the positive impact on shareholders is justifiably very motivating. This momentum deserves our full recognition. This article explores TRX Gold's current situation and growth strategy.

The context is a friendly one. Robust demand for safe-haven assets in the face of uncertainty and instability due to macroeconomic problems and geopolitical chaos, as well as growing hopes for interest rate cuts by the Federal Reserve, resulted in the Gold Spot Price (XAUUSD:CUR) reaching $5,095.68 per ounce at the time of writing, up 17.7% year to date and 85.7% over the past 12 months. Amid this golden hype, TRX shares have risen by 51% since the beginning of the year and by 345.4% in the last 12 months.

TRX Gold Corporation: Success With Normal Effort

The stock is listed on the prestigious NYSE, where its rising market value is driven by investors looking to benefit from growth with minimal effort while avoiding the complexities of direct investment in physical gold. For these investors, the formula seems simple: the price of gold rises, and given its unprecedented climb, TRX Gold Corporation’s robust profitability—much to the delight of shareholders—is within reach, thanks to a management team that is keeping the company on a winning trajectory. As long as this positive trend continues, we completely understand whether investors seek to strengthen this team even further.

According to trading data from Seeking Alpha, TRX Gold's average trading volume over the past three months was 2.14 million shares as of this writing. Take a look at the “Trading Data” from Seeking Alpha. This makes it a low liquid stock on the market, but not as illiquid as many other gold stocks we examined in our former analysis of this sector.

Low liquidity has its drawbacks: In the event of a sudden price drop, it can be difficult to sell shares quickly. The need to lower the selling price of a trade can, in the worst-case scenario, lead to poor performance or even losses. As is well known, the world is diverse and multifaceted, and the TRX stock market is no exception. Market participants may desire certain characteristics of TRX Gold shares that could elevate their satisfaction to new heights. Paradoxically, some investors even prefer lower daily trading volumes, as the upward momentum of TRX Gold, driven by the all-time high in the gold price, could be even stronger in a market with fewer available shares, intensifying competition for the stocks with the most promising growth prospects.

This group of investors is, deep inside, really hoping that TRX Gold’s balance sheet continues to strengthen, as it appears to be doing so far, and will allow the company to enact the buyback of a bulk of its own shares on the open market. Such a move could create a turbo effect and add further value to the market—especially at a time when liquidity, stability, and efficiency are in high demand amid the considerable uncertainty and volatility caused by geopolitical tensions. And since these very tensions are fueling the ongoing gold boom, which shows no signs of slowing down and is relentlessly striving for its next peak, all of this only reinforces the regret that daily trading volume isn't even lower.

About TRX Gold Corporation: A High-Margin Growing Gold Producer in Tanzania

TRX Gold Corporation presents itself in its Q1 2026 report for the three months ending November 30, 2025, as follows:

TRX Gold is a high margin and growing gold company advancing the Buckreef Gold Project in Tanzania. Buckreef Gold includes an established open pit operation and 2,000 tonnes per day process plant with upside potential demonstrated in the May 2025 PEA.

Clarification 1: Through its subsidiary “TRX Gold Tanzania Limited,” TRX Gold Corporation holds a 55% interest in the joint venture “Buckreef Gold Corporation Limited,” which operates the Buckreef Gold Project. The remaining 45% is owned by the Tanzanian government through its agency STAMICO.

Clarification 2: The Buckreef Mine is located in Tanzania, 4 km northwest of Lwamgasa and near the Lake Victoria Goldfields. The region has a long history of gold mining, although large parts of the area remain largely unexplored. The pre-modern colonial period was the most significant and intensive phase (see “Lake Victoria Goldfields,” published in Episodes (2016) by J. Henckel et al.). At that time, neither commodity prices nor mining and exploration technologies had advanced to the point where they offered strong incentives, as they do today. Thanks to changing market conditions and technological advances, these deposits have once again become attractive to gold exploration companies. Equipped with modern mining technologies and benefiting from a significant increase in gold prices, these Tanzanian deposits—despite generally exhibiting a low precious metal concentration per tonne of ore—are now competitive on the global market.

Clarification 3: TRX Gold Corporation's fiscal year runs from September 1st to August 31st. Therefore, fiscal year 2026 began on September 1st, 2025, and ends on August 31st, 2026. The quarter from September 1st, 2025, to November 30th, 2025, is thus the first quarter of fiscal year 2026.

Clarification 4: TRX Gold Corporation is headquartered in Oakville, Ontario, Canada.

TRX Gold Corporation’s Q1 2026 report adds:

The PEA outlines average gold production of 62,000 oz per annum over 17.6 years at 3,000 tonnes per day of throughput capacity, and $1.9 billion pre-tax NPV 5% at average life of mine gold price of $4,000/oz 2.

The Buckreef Gold Project hosts a Measured and Indicated Mineral Resource of 10.8 million tonnes (“MT”) at 2.57 grams per tonne (“g/t”) gold containing 893,000 ounces (“oz”) of gold and an Inferred Mineral Resource of 9.1 MT at 2.47 g/t gold for 726,000 oz of gold.

About TRX Gold Corporation: The Commitment to Returning Value

We previously mentioned the possibility that TRX Gold could return value through a share buyback, thereby creating the conditions for that upward pressure in daily trading that could benefit shares on the NYSE.

This could very well happen precisely during the peak of organic growth, which TRX Gold is pursuing as a business strategy.

In fact, in its Q1 2026 report, TRX Gold Corporation adds:

The leadership team is focused on creating both near-term and long-term shareholder value by increasing gold production to generate positive cash flow to fund the expansion as outlined in the PEA and grow Mineral Resources through exploration. TRX Gold’s actions are informed by the highest environmental, social and corporate governance (“ESG”) standards, as evidenced by the relationships and programs that the Company has developed during its nearly two decades of presence in the Geita Region, Tanzania.

Share buybacks can reduce trading volume and liquidity by decreasing the free float, but their impact depends on the scale. In the case of TRX Gold, the current buyback program is too small relative to the outstanding shares, free float, and daily trading volume to produce the much-desired effect on liquidity. The company's announced buybacks totalled between $0.3 million and $0.9 million over the past year. The largest buyback, at $0.9 million (LTM ended November 30, 2025), involved repurchasing approximately 650,000 shares at current market values of the shares—just 0.23% of the 286.96 million shares outstanding or S.O. (see “S.O.” figure “TRX Trading Data”). This equates to roughly 2,600 shares per day, which is negligible compared to the average daily trading volume of 2.14 million (as previously mentioned). This illustrates the minimal impact on liquidity. Nevertheless, share buybacks are important and popular in the market because they signal management's confidence in its corporate strategy and ultimately still have the same positive effect on the share price as with illiquid gold stocks (or those that are much less liquid than TRX Gold), which, for example, do not buy back their own shares on the market. Even with minimal impact on the free float and market liquidity, TRX Gold investors should continue to monitor future share buybacks and reasonably wait for a positive effect on the value of their positions. TRX Gold owns only 55% of the total gold production from the Buckreef mine in Tanzania, further reducing its share of the market supply in ounces. But this is precisely where the appeal of this gold stock lies: the company achieves high profit margins and can finance a growth project that is expected to secure production for at least two decades. The upside potential is fueled by TRX Gold's management, which has succeeded in extracting maximum value from the limited ounces and generating the greatest possible value from the exceptional gold. As a result, there are solid reasons for optimism about the creation of shareholder value.

TRX Gold Reports First Quarter 2026 Results

Operationally, TRX Gold looked like this in the first quarter of 2026.

Operations

In his commentary on the report for the first quarter of 2026 (the three months ending November 30, 2025) dated January 15, 2026, Stephen Mullowney, CEO of TRX Gold, stated:

In Q1, we once again delivered record results, in line with guidance shared last quarter, pouring a record 6,597 ounces of gold and selling 6,492 ounces of gold at an average realized price of $3,860 per ounce, […]

Gold Production: A record 6,597 ounces of gold were poured in Q1 FY 2026, and production is growing, reflecting a 36.3% year-on-year advancement from 4,841 ounces in Q1 FY 2025. This growth is attributable to improved strip ratios, a doubling of the ore volume mined, and higher-than-planned gold grades. The plant is being used more intensively, and the ongoing expansion and modernisation of the existing processing plant to 3,000 tons per day, as well as the conversion from open-pit to underground mining over the next three years, require additional investments. The company does not view these expenditures as a problem or uncertainty, but rather as a clear and realistic step towards profitability, as long as gold prices continue to ratchet up. The operating costs are more than justified, as the record gold price is being skillfully leveraged. This not only compensates for the additional capital expenditures completely but also enables the efficient implementation of the gold expansion program in Tanzania. Otherwise, TRX Gold would not generate any free cash flow at this stage while simultaneously establishing a solid foundation for future, internally generated cash flows.

Financials

Financially, TRX Gold looked like this in the first quarter of 2026.

In his commentary on the report for the first quarter of 2026 (the three months ending November 30, 2025) dated January 15, 2026, Stephen Mullowney, CEO of TRX Gold, stated:

[…] at an average realized price of $3,860 per ounce, generating revenue of $25.1 million, gross profit of $14.2 million (57% margin) and EBITDA 1 of $13.2 million (53% margin). The strong cash flow in a record gold price environment has enabled us to meaningfully reinvest in TRX Gold’s growth. […]

On a Year-on-year basis.

  • Revenue went up 100.5% to $25.117 million.
  • Adjusted income went up 313% to $7.732 million.
  • Adjusted EBITDA went up 200.7% to $13.211 million.
  • Operating Cash Flow went up 69% to $4.02 million.
  • Margin (avg. realised price net–cash cost) went up 89% to $2,352/oz.

Accelerated Growth With Organically Generated Funds

Stephen Mullowney, CEO of TRX Gold, added:

[…] During the quarter, we strengthened our working capital position, advanced plans to upgrade and expand our processing plant to improve our production profile, and increased investment in exploration to further delineate resources at Buckreef Gold. The Company is entering a new phase of growth, with improving production scale, stronger margins and a growing resource base. We are very encouraged by the progress and excited about the year ahead.

The company is accelerating the expansion and modernisation of its processing plant to a capacity of 3,000 tonnes per day. This will equip the operation for average annual gold production of over 62,000 ounces—more than double the 25,000 to 30,000 ounces projected for fiscal year 2026. The expansion will be financed from internal cash flow, in parallel with ongoing mine development projects, including the long-term transition from open-pit to underground mining. New drill targets are already being identified, and metallurgical test work is fueling expectations of positive recoveries. Markets are expecting important news from these fronts, which is likely to be released in the second quarter of fiscal year 2026. The expansion is fueling gold inventories, which stood at 22,891 ounces as of January 15, 2026. Its value, which is well-positioned given rising gold prices, represents a valuable additional source of liquidity and complements the cash and cash equivalents of $9.2 million as of November 30, 2025, as well as the cash flow generated from operations, while TRX Gold Corp. only had debts of $4.6 million as of November 30, 2025.

Let’s say that, as in Q1 FY 2026, the expansion will need to allocate $4 million, including CapEx, quarterly.

Free Cash Flow Estimate for Rest of FY 2026

In the first quarter of fiscal year 2026, the company achieved an average selling price of $3,860 per ounce for 6,597 ounces, had cash costs of $1,508 per ounce, and thus generated a cash inflow from operations of $4.02 million, or $609.40 per ounce. In addition to the cash cost of $1,508 per ounce, further costs of approximately $1,743 per ounce were incurred, likely related to the scale-up of production ($1,743 = ($3,860/oz minus $1,508/oz) minus $609.40/oz). Total costs thus amounted to $3,250 per ounce for producing and selling one ounce of gold in the first quarter of fiscal year 2026 ($3,250 = total cash cost of $1,508/oz plus additional expenditures of nearly $1,743/oz).

Based on the fiscal year 2026 forecast—a production target of 25,000 to 30,000 ounces of gold at an average cash cost of $1,400 to $1,600 per ounce for Buckreef Gold—we anticipate the company will produce approximately 6,875 ounces per quarter at cash costs of $1,500 per ounce. We are assuming all other conditions remain unchanged, and total costs are about $3,243 per ounce ($3,243/oz = avg. cash cost of $1,500/oz plus $1,743/oz). We expect the company to generate an operating cash inflow of approximately $13.8 million per quarter, or an average of $2,002 per ounce, based on an average gold price of $5,245 per ounce. This figure of estimated gold price/oz falls between the current level of approximately $5,085 per ounce and Goldman Sachs' forecast of $5,400 per ounce.

Thus,

$2,002/oz = $5,245/oz - $3,243/oz.

$2,002 multiplied by 6,875 ounces per quarter leads to an operating cash inflow of $13.8 million per quarter.

Assuming an investment of approximately $4 million per quarter, as it was in Q1 FY 2026 (“cash from investing”), including CapEx, this results in an average free cash flow of approximately $9.8 million per quarter.

If the company maintains the same allocation ratio as in the last 12 months through November 30, 2025, and uses 30% of its free cash flow for common share repurchases, TRX Gold could potentially allocate $3 million to common share repurchases. At the current share price, this equates to 2.2 million shares bought back per quarter or 35,200 shares daily. Although this buyback volume still appears moderate compared to the market liquidity of 2.14 million shares (as previously mentioned), it signals a stronger and more substantial commitment to returning capital to shareholders through share buybacks. Overall, considering these forecasts and assumptions, TRX Gold's potential share buyback program is likely to become an important component of its future capital allocation strategy and should have a positive impact on the share price.

TRX Gold Corporation on the NYSE

TRX Gold Corporation’s NYSE-listed shares under the symbol “TRX” were trading at $1.39/share, giving it a market capitalisation of $395.51 million, as of the writing of this section of the article.

Technical Analysis

The current stock price is 59.8% above its 52-week average of $0.84 (range: $0.27 to $1.47). The 20-day average is above the 50-day average and above the 100-day average, which in turn is above the 200-day average. TRX stock is in an upward trend not only in the short term but also in the medium and long term, indicating that the market positively assesses the company's improving prospects. The RSI of 0.78x indicates that the stock is trading in a range near overbought territory. We think many shareholders would be happy with a temporary price correction to strengthen their positions and are waiting for one. The problem, however, is that the price of gold continues to rise, and frankly, even with the best intentions, it's hard to imagine the stock price falling sharply from here. But everything is possible with stock markets.

Valuation

This stock still seems cheap despite the rally.

If TRX Gold generates an operating cash flow of $13.8 million per quarter, this translates to a 12-month forward cash flow of $55.2 million. The ratio of market price to 12-month forward cash flow is then 7.2x compared to the sector median of 11.80x.

Seeking Alpha Analysts reports an EV/EBITDA (FWD) of 3.84x for TRX Gold, compared to the sector median of 10.06x, as of this writing.

Regarding the economic estimates of the Preliminary Economic Assessment (“PEA”) for the Tanzanian operations' expansion project, TRX Gold's Q1 2026 report states:

Base case NPV 5% of $701.0 million pre-tax, or $442.2 million after tax at consensus forecast case gold prices ($2,707/oz year 1, $2,646/oz year 2, $2,495/oz year 3, $2,400/oz year 4, $2,245/oz thereafter).

This means that the net present value is approximately $930 million, assuming an average gold price of $5,250 per ounce, as used to estimate future free cash flow. The NPV of $930 million or $3.24 per share is 2.33 times the current market valuation.

The Risk Section

Gold and exploration activities in the mineral resources sector in Tanzania are not really risk-free, as the Sprott Mining Risk Heat Map assigns an orange code, reflecting a high operational risk there.

The risk also lies in the price of gold. Should it suddenly fall, everything would have to be reassessed. TRX Gold might then have less confidence in its generated free cash flow, which could negatively impact the stock's sentiment. Currently, this risk is very low to extremely low, but as you know, nothing is certain in the market.

Conclusion

TRX Gold Corporation offers an excellent investment opportunity in the gold sector. The company already boasts a high profit margin and, thanks to its internally financed growth, is fueled by the record price of the precious metal and enjoys friendly conditions to scale up further in the near future. This stock is not so illiquid that a sudden augment in the number of participants demanding its shares would lead to a drastic price rally. But the market can expect a higher value through share buybacks, and this could be sufficient to drive the share price a lot higher from current levels.

data.title: RA: 10% Yield Not As Good As It Looks
data.description:
Brookfield Real Assets Income Fund Inc. (RA) offers a tempting dividend yield over 10%. The fund is invested mostly in fixed-income of real estate and infrastructure. While these sound like durable assets, I think there are weaknesses in their approach and I only rate it Hold.

Concept of the Fund

RA is an actively managed, closed-end fund that aims for total returns, primarily through investment income of the managed portfolio.

Brookfield does this primarily by investing in the credit of real estate, infrastructure, and natural resources. In essence, RA is a fixed-income vehicle and something of a bet on these types of assets. A modest portion, usually no more than 10%, is invested in the equities of these assets or companies that operate them. As these equities are dividend-paying, they are consistent with the income focus of the portfolio.

The fund often utilizes leverage at about 25% of total assets. Most of this is through their credit facility, but about a third is through reverse repurchase agreement, which carry margin call risk. This risk factor is likely why they keep their total leverage contained. Even without margin call risk, leverage has the effect of enhancing the yield on securities, while also intensifying losses over time.

Leverage is likely used because the weighted average coupon on the fixed-income is low, 5.26% as of Q3. This is in spite of the fact that the assets are, by and large, below investment-grade or unrated.

History and Performance

Since inception in 2016, the fund has shown extreme sensitivity to drawdowns, with two major ones occurring in 2020 and then in 2023.

2020's was a result of the COVID crash. NAV sustained big hits, as real estate and infrastructure assets became inactive or underused during the lockdowns and quarantining. COVID stimulus and low interest rates spurred a recovery, but continued weakness in commercial real estate materialized in 2023's drawdown as well.

While 2020 was a sharper drop, 2023's distress was more meaningful. The monthly dividend fell from $0.199 to $0.081 per share, prompting a decline in the share price that has not recovered.

Looking at the dividend, we that a large portion, sometimes almost half of the distribution, is return of capital. While the underlying portfolio income does provide some yield, there is also a regular tendency to trim some assets and return it as capital.

Remembering that total returns are a goal too, those have been positive in the life of this fund, but the impact of the drawdowns is still quite large. This shows that even a modestly leverage portfolio can have some intensified losses from leverage.

Outlook and Valuation

RA currently shows some signs of undervaluation. The first indication is the current NAV discount.

The current discount to NAV is about 10%, which I find is often when we could start to consider a CEF on NAV discount alone. The dividend yield at these prices is 10.88% as well.

There may also be a boon to their portfolio coming soon. The term of Federal Reserve Chairman Jerome Powell is nearing its end, and President Trump is eyeing candidates that will cut rates more aggressively. Interest rate relief would likely benefit a portfolio exposed to real estate and infrastructure, as such companies are typically very leveraged and sensitive to interest rate cycles.

Thinking more long-term, however, particularly as their weighted average coupon isn't terribly high, this suggests lower-coupon securities will replace maturing issues in their portfolio.

At the same time, I don't think the coupons they get are reasonable for the credit ratings of the securities. While some of this is a consequence of their short duration (about three years), RA is a not a short-term security, and being stuck with the yields of shorter maturities is a key weakness in this fund.

While most of the 3.1% expense ratio is interest expense from the leverage, there's still a 1% management fee. Given the performance, as well as the limited upside on a fixed-income-heavy portfolio, this strikes me as unnecessary friction on the ability of shareholders to reinvest and compound their shares over time. The tendency to trim assets to meet certain yields also risks weaker NAV over time.

Realistically, I think equity investments in these assets, as opposed to fixed-income, would likely be much more attractive over time.

Conclusion

Overall, there's reason to think RA may be on the other side of a down cycle, facilitated by changing Federal Reserve policy. I don't see it as a dependable income vehicle, and as total returns go, I suspect the portfolio is vulnerable to setbacks, given the leverage (if modest) used to purchase low-yield, "junk" bonds. At best, I suspect it will give index-live returns over time.

data.title: Taiwan Semiconductor: The AI Boom Is Coming To An End
data.description:
Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company (TSM) recently reported one of its strongest quarters in history, with record revenue, margin expansion, and a dramatically improved growth outlook driven by demand for AI. The results for fiscal Q4 2025 and guidance for Q1 2026 confirm that TSMC remains a central infrastructure provider for the global AI revolution, with leading-edge nodes and advanced packaging operating at very high utilization rates.

However, in our view, these results also mark the late stage of the current cycle. The stock is currently priced as if peak margins, peak utilization, and continued demand growth driven by AI will continue for many years. At the same time, TSMC is committed to unprecedented levels of capital spending, increased geopolitical and regulatory exposure, and structurally increasing its fixed cost base. While short-term fundamentals remain strong, we believe the risk-reward profile has become unfavorable at current valuation levels. We maintain our Sell rating.

Peak Results Reflect an Exceptionally Favorable Environment

TSMC's Q4 2025 results beat even the most optimistic expectations. Revenue reached $33.7 billion, up 25.5% year-over-year, while net income rose 35% to $16 billion. Gross margin expanded to 62.3%, driven by high utilization of advanced nodes, favorable resource mix, and productivity gains. Advanced technologies at 7 nm and below accounted for 77% of wafer revenue, with high-performance computing accounting for 55% of total sales.

The outlook for Q1 2026 points to continued sequential growth, with revenue expected to be between $34.6 billion and $35.8 billion and gross margin between 63% and 65%. Management also reiterated its expectation for revenue growth of nearly 30% in 2026, significantly outpacing the foundry industry as a whole. These figures clearly demonstrate TSMC's dominant competitive position and pricing power in the current environment.

However, these results reflect a near-perfect combination of strong demand, tight supply, and favorable cost absorption. Historically, periods of peak utilization and record profitability in capital-intensive industries have tended to be temporary, rather than structural.

Capital Intensity Is Rising Meaningfully

The most important takeaway from the latest earnings report was a sharp increase in planned capital expenditures. TSMC now expects capital expenditures in 2026 to be between $52 billion and $56 billion, up from about $41 billion in 2025. About 70% to 80% of this budget will be allocated to advanced process technologies, with an additional 10% to 20% going to advanced packaging and related infrastructure.

While these investments are necessary to support customer demand, they significantly increase TSMC's operating leverage. Depreciation is expected to increase at a high rate in 2026, while overseas fabs are expected to reduce gross margins by 2% to 3% initially and up to 3% to 4% going forward. Additionally, the rollout of 2nm technology is expected to further pressure margins in the second half of 2026.

As a result, even small deviations from current demand assumptions could have an excessive impact on profitability and free cash flow. In our view, the market is underestimating the downside sensitivity caused by this higher fixed cost structure.

AI Concentration and Demand Risk

Management remains confident that AI demand is a multi-year structural trend, not a cyclical surge. AI accelerator revenue is expected to grow at a mid-to-high 50% CAGR through 2029, as leading customers continue to request additional capacity. However, this also means that a growing portion of TSMC's revenue and capital allocation is tied to a relatively narrow set of hyperscalers and AI platforms.

Any slowdown in AI infrastructure spending, model efficiency optimization, or suspension of hyperscaler CapEx could quickly shift the supply-demand balance. Notably, management acknowledged that new capacity additions will not significantly ease supply constraints until 2028 or later, meaning that the current pricing power is being carried forward while the cost burden is being shifted to the latter.

This creates the risk that TSMC will enter a period of severe depreciation and margin pressure just as industry demand growth is normalizing.

Geopolitical and Regulatory Exposure Is Increasing

TSMC's global expansion strategy adds another layer of risk. The company is accelerating investments in the United States, Japan, and Europe, partly driven by customer demand and partly by political considerations. Recent trade agreements have the potential to reduce tariffs, but they also create ongoing uncertainty about future policy changes, local cost structures, and government expectations.

U.S. manufacturing, in particular, remains structurally less efficient than manufacturing in Taiwan. While management has made progress on productivity, the long-term margin impact of operating multiple clusters of gigafactory plants in different regions remains uncertain. As overseas capacity increases, execution risk increases, and flexibility decreases.

The Real Value of TSMC

To find out TMSC's fair value, we built a valuation model at a time when the stock was trading at $334.87 per share. The model assumes a 3% terminal growth rate, consistent with the rate that we used when valuing other semiconductor companies. Our forecast covers the next 5 years and incorporates inputs such as cash and total debt from the most recent earnings report.

The model applies a discount rate of 8.56%, derived from TSMC's estimated cost of equity and cost of debt. The cost of debt was based primarily on TTM data, while the cost of equity was calculated using the CAPM method with a risk-free rate of 4.23%, a beta of 1.27, and a market return rate of 7.77%.

Our revenue and EBIT projections for TSMC over the next 5 years are broadly similar to the overall consensus estimates. We also anticipate a gradual decline in EBIT margins, as macroeconomic headwinds and seasonal fluctuations could weigh on profitability. In our model, we also applied a 15% tax rate, in line with the assumptions used in our prior valuation models. We see projected CAPEX at around 54 billion in 2026, which is close to the management estimates. The change in net working capital is modeled at 5% of sales. Based on these inputs, our model estimates TSMC's enterprise value to be $1.05 trillion and its equity value to be $1.12 trillion, which implies an intrinsic value of $215.66 per share.

Risks to Our Bearish Thesis

There are scenarios where our sell-side thesis may not hold. AI demand could remain stronger for longer than expected, allowing TSMC to maintain high utilization and pricing power over the next decade. Productivity gains and strategic pricing could offset some of the margin erosion from overseas expansion. In addition, TSMC's technology leadership remains unrivaled, limiting competitive threats in the near term.

If revenue continues to grow at around 30% annually and margins remain above the long-term target of 56%, the valuation compression could be delayed.

Final Thoughts

TSMC is performing exceptionally well in an environment characterized by high demand for AI, tight supply, and record prices. The fourth-quarter results and first-quarter guidance show the company's dominance in advanced manufacturing and packaging. However, these results also reflect peak cycle conditions supported by factors that are unlikely to remain so favorable indefinitely. Given the sharp increase in capital spending, rising geopolitical influence, and continued valuation integrity, we believe that downside risks now outweigh incremental upside. While TSMC remains a high-quality company, the stock appears fully valued in the context of heightened cyclical risk. At current levels, we see a more attractive risk-reward trade-off in risk aversion and reaffirm our Sell rating.

data.title: The Quantum Pair Trade: Long IonQ, Short Quantum Computing
data.description:
Once upon a time, I used to work in New York City as a professional proprietary trader - that is, someone who works for a private trading company, taking risk in the markets and getting paid a cut of what I produce from my trading account.

While I primarily focused on positive theta option trading, my colleagues on the desk deployed strategies that varied across asset classes, time horizons, and financial edges.

In many ways, when I worked as a professional trader, it cut my learning curve on almost every type of trading style imaginable, especially one that I always find myself coming back to: long/short equities.

Long short portfolio management, for those who don’t know, is a style of trading where an investor selects a number of positions to go long on, and a number of positions to go short on - all at the same time.

The idea is that by pairing trades against one another, investors can hedge out market and industry risk while exposing themselves - purely - to one business’s execution versus another.

It’s a style of trading that I’ve always found intuitive, although I don’t tend to employ it much given the importance of overall market beta as a return driver.

That said, sometimes I stumble across a trade idea that looks just too good to pass up.

In this article, I'll break a recent idea I had down in detail: long IonQ (IONQ), short Quantum Computing Inc (QUBT).

While both of these stocks are widely known as pure play quantum computing businesses, IONQ has material revenues, a credible board of directors, and a feasible commercialization strategy. Sure, the business is speculatively priced, but I view the growth trajectory here as considerable.

On the other hand, QUBT is a smaller quantum player that has struggled with commercialization, produces meager revenues, and trades at a patently absurd valuation.
