With a rocky corporate history, sub-scale manufacturing, and significant doubts around management’s ability to commercialize, the stock is a perfect short complement to a long IONQ position.

Today, I’ll dive deeper into each of these stocks, outline their position, and make the case that a 'long IONQ, short QUBT' trade could produce significant alpha over the next few years.

Sound good? Let’s dive in.

IonQ

Let’s begin with IONQ, the proposed long leg of the aforementioned pair trade.

In short, IonQ is a pure play quantum computing company aiming to produce fully commercialized quantum computing, sensing, networking, and security solutions for businesses and government partners in the coming years.

In recent quarters, the company has been executing on this exact goal. Over the last 12 months, IonQ has produced nearly $80 million in revenue, more than 50% of which it kept as gross profit:

The company’s SG&A and R&D expenses are - understandably - massive, leading to negative operating results, but significant revenue acceleration, especially in the most recent quarter, highlights just how much progress the company is making in commercializing its technology:

The company's results in the most recent quarter soared significantly above guidance and analyst consensus, highlighting that demand for the company’s computing solutions is growing rapidly:

IonQ delivered its largest quarterly revenue beat ever, delivering results 37% above the high end of our guidance. Putting this in perspective, we grew revenue year-on-year by 222%, a truly impressive number that demonstrates our commercial traction. Our company's growth trajectory is clear, and we have only just scratched the surface of the enormous opportunities we are pursuing.

Not only that, but the company continues to build momentum by bringing in institutional caliber board members and hurdling significant technological challenges.

On the board member side, General Jay Raymond recently joined the board of directors, being the only four-star general since 1947 to have served in two separate branches of the Armed Forces. He also founded the US military’s Space Force during President Trump’s first term. This should give IonQ inroads into the lucrative government contracting market, while also bringing in significant organizational expertise that management can lean on.

On the technological side, the company recently announced that their AQ 64 Tempo system was up and running three months ahead of schedule. Not only is this a significant execution win, but this new Tempo system will bring unprecedented speed to linear computational problems like drug development, cybersecurity, and more, implying a significant revenue opportunity for the company.

Last year, in partnership with Nvidia (NVDA), Amazon (AMZN), and AstraZeneca (AZN), IonQ demonstrated the world’s first 20x quantum speedup in computational drug development using the company’s Forte system. Now, with Tempo having a computational space ~260 million times larger than Forte, this new computing system (expected to ship in 2026) could buttress the company’s revenue momentum:

Our newest system, which we unveiled at our Analyst Day on September 12 is called Tempo. Public benchmarks show Tempo has a compute space 36 quadrillion times larger than the leading commercial superconducting system in the market. We are proud that Tempo is scheduled to ship in 2026 and has a computational space approximately 260 million times larger than our current fully commercialized Forte system.

With a quantum edge in a commercially available system like this, IonQ also has a significant 'land and expand' opportunity into networking, sensing, and other quantum opportunities, which explains IonQ’s recent acquisition of Vector Atomic. This M&A deal positions IonQ to capture significant value from dollars flowing into the quantum ecosystem.

Overall, in my view, the company’s future is bright.

Valuation wise, the stock is quite expensive, which can be seen as a combination of the market understanding the opportunity, combined with a hefty speculation premium.

Right now, with less than $80 million in sales and trading at a market cap of more than $16 billion, IonQ trades at a price to sales ratio of more than 150x:

This is more expensive than Palantir, for reference.

Analyst estimates help make sense of this revenue growth somewhat, with consensus estimates putting the company’s 2030 revenue at roughly $1.55 billion. Of course, there’s no question that 10x 2030 sales is expensive, but given the broad growth opportunity, I view shares of IonQ as speculatively charged, not prohibitively expensive.

Quantum Computing Inc.

On the flip side, we have Quantum Computing Inc., a roughly $2.5 billion market cap company that produces almost nothing in revenues and has no real economics, financials, margins, or commercialization of any kind.

In previous articles, I’ve spoken about my doubts around the company’s market fit and overall commercialization strategy:

Since that reorganization, QUBT has begun work on its quantum computing business, which primarily focuses on 9 key products and services, including Dirac-3, the company's 'high-performance quantum machine':

In our view, this product lineup is a bit confusing, which reflects the seemingly 'scattershot' approach of management to find product-market fit.

To some degree, the company has quelled these issues with a stronger commercial vision as of late, but at a base level, QUBT is a hyper speculative quantum company that claims it has widely validated quantum computing processes across use cases:

Our long-term goal is to move from prototype and small batch manufacturing towards volume production. And we see that transition take shape by the end of this decade. To get there, our current 3-year road map is focused on refining our processes, scaling small batch production and expanding our team and facility to position QCi for industry scale output. In other words, the technology is there. Our quantum machines and photonic chips have been validated across multiple use cases. The next step is to scale the engineering and the manufacturing behind them, and we now have the team, resources, facility and a plan to make that happen.

At the same time, management has sold roughly $1.5 billion worth of shares onto the open market over the last few quarters, giving QUBT a massive war chest that theoretically could be used to jumpstart its commercialization plans.

My issue with the company is that there's no money coming in the door to back the strategy up:

In my mind, QUBT has had enough time and money to begin truly executing on the opportunity it says it has, but revenue growth remains sluggish, and the company has not yet even produced $1 million in TTM sales. What's the hold up?

Combine this with the fact that QUBT’s corporate name in 2018 was 'Innovative Beverage Group Holdings', and I’m not imbued with a sense of overall confidence that management will be able to make substantial revenues off their technology, especially in light of IonQ’s recent progress, along with other quantum players like IBM and Google.

On the valuation side, if you thought IonQ was expensive, QUBT appears downright absurd, trading with an inflated price to sales multiple of roughly 3200x.

Analyst consensus estimates put the company’s 2027 revenue at roughly $15 million, but even assuming growth like this, it doesn’t do much to compress the multiple:

Assuming analyst estimates are accurate - and in my view they seem potentially quite optimistic - shares of QUBT will be trading at a 2027 sales multiple of roughly 171x.

This is more expensive than shares of IonQ trade at, currently.

Why A Pair Trade?

So - taken together, with IONQ, we have a promising (if somewhat speculative) stock, and with QUBT, we have a hyper speculative stock that has a history of massive share sales and very little in the way of revenue.

Why go long one and short the other as a pair, as opposed to simply investing in long and short positions outright?

In my view, I see pair trading as a convenient way to manage hyper volatility in stocks that have considerable speculative premiums; especially stocks that are part of a hot narrative.

In other words, buying and holding IonQ might be the right move over a long period of time, but the amount of volatility that the stock will likely endure throughout that period seems considerable - and potentially unbearable.

At the same time, shorting QUBT alone is a risky proposition, especially considering how these quantum stocks can spike dramatically overnight on news headlines.

By pairing them together and allocating 50% to one and 50% to the other, your capital no longer has meaningful exposure to the stock market or quantum computing as an industry. Any move driven purely by quantum computing as a theme will affect both stocks equally, thus reducing the overall impact on your P&L.

With a trade like this, you are purely exposed to IonQ, its management, its execution, and its valuation, which then compete directly against QUBT, its management, and its valuation.

Given the arguments I've laid out in the above sections, I think this is an incredibly attractive way to make money in an otherwise difficult market. At present, borrowing shares of QUBT to short charges an annual interest rate of roughly 0.5%, making the cost of putting on this trade somewhat negligible.

It's very difficult to outline what the risk / reward for a pair trade like this might be, but with $900 million in book value, QUBT could have 60%+ downside from here.

For IONQ, the upside is equally foggy, but it wouldn't surprise me to see shares trading 10% - 30% higher a year from now as quantum momentum builds.

Add this potential performance up, while hedging out market and sector swings, and you can see why I'm so interested in this trade idea.

Summary

Overall, I believe going long IonQ and short QUBT is an incredibly attractive pair trade that hedges out key risks, while retaining upside to my individual views on each stock.

By taking this trade, you expose yourself to a best in breed pure play quantum operator with material headway on commercialization, while laying off risk using a company that seems more interested in selling shares of stock than working products.

Cheers~

data.title: United Parcel Service: 2026 Is The Year To Accumulate
data.description:
Introduction

United Parcel Service, Inc. (NYSE: UPS) has posted a strong return since I rated it a Strong Buy in late October, as it has increased by nearly 16% since. With the company releasing its latest earnings report, I want to dive into the famed enterprise and see if the rally can continue to have legs based on the huge turnaround that the company has been undergoing.

Current Dynamics

I’ll start with the biggest news, the recent earnings release. The company showed a resilient picture as it is currently managing the Amazon (AMZN) glide-down relatively well. Consolidated revenue came in at $24.5B, a slight dip compared to the previous peak seasons, but nevertheless a beat of $490MM against the consensus. It is also demonstrating the company’s goal to shed unprofitable volume. Adjusted operating margin landed at 11.8%, further enhancing the cost-cutting measures, which are starting to improve the bottom line. On the EPS front, the company posted a Q4 Non-GAAP of $2.38, a beat of 18 cents.

Segment-wise, it is important to note that the international segment was the standout performer, growing 2.5% to reach $5.05B and the highest Q4 revenue in four years. This has pushed adjusted margins to a massive 18%. On the U.S. side, though, revenue fell 3.2% to $16.76B as volume dropped 10.8%. But interestingly enough, revenue per piece rose by 8.3%, again showing how the strategy is taking hold as UPS is charging more for better-quality packages. On the supply chain front, revenue fell 12.7% to $2.68B due mainly to weakness in the mail innovations business, but operating margins actually expanded to 10.3% thanks to automation.

Now, CEO Carol Tomé has been very vocal about 2026 being an inflection point due to the Amazon exit, and the company has successfully reduced its reliance on the tech giant to what it calls healthy levels. This shifts UPS away from low-margin deliveries and has allowed UPS to free up space for more profitable B2B and Healthcare shipments. On the labor side, the company confirmed that 48,000 operational positions were eliminated in 2025 alone, replaced by high-velocity automated hubs. This was part of the network of the future strategy, which is an entire physical overhaul of how the company moves packages. It has been opening volume centers, which are highly automated hubs using AI and robotics to sort packages. The company also completed the retirement of its MD-11 aircraft fleet this quarter, the famed tri-reactor planes, thus pushing UPS towards a more fuel-efficient and modernized air network that should improve profitability in the long run.

Looking ahead, UPS provided a bullish outlook for the coming year, which is what truly drove the stock price higher today. The full-year revenue goal is set to reach $89.7B, which is nearly $2 higher than what the street was expecting. More focused, the company is on track to hit $11.2B in annual healthcare-related revenue, moving toward its goal of becoming the world’s #1 complex healthcare logistics provider. That’s why it acquired the Canada-based Andlauer Healthcare Group for $1.6B earlier in November. This will add a massive network of temperature-controlled facilities and cold-chain transport capabilities, as UPS is betting that shipping life-saving biologics and specialized medicine will be more profitable than shipping discount blenders for major retailers.

Valuation

UPS currently has a P/E GAAP of 16.52, which is in line with its most direct peers, FedEx (FDX) at 16.78 and DHL (DHLGY) at 15.23. On a FCF basis, despite the continued investments to improve margins and automation, the company is still posting a relatively strong FCF yield at 5.1%. The kicker to owning the company’s share would be the current dividend yield, which is handsomely rewarding shareholders for waiting for the current strategy to take hold. I’ll also say that it is relatively safe considering the cost savings that are expected to reach $3B in the second half of 2026 and also the capex reductions of $3B. That’s why management is confident and has raised its dividend by 1 cent to keep its streak alive.

Looking at the growth prospects for UPS, I’m particularly bullish on the healthcare push with the company targeting $20B in annual healthcare revenue by the end of 2026, which would represent nearly 20% of the entire company’s sales. As UPS's size and sector of activity have now made it a proxy for the health of the economy, adding a sector to its utility belt that is recession-proof would provide a great buffer. UPS's strategy to replace Amazon’s revenue is also smart, as it is aggressively courting SMBs through its Digital Access Program, which has pushed the volume up to nearly 33% of the company’s U.S. segment, up from under 27% a couple of years ago. The integration of the shipping tools directly into e-commerce platforms such as Shopify (SHOP) will make it harder for small businesses to switch to competitors. UPS can also charge higher retail rates than it did for Amazon.

Tying into this, I’m particularly enthusiastic about the company’s revenue per piece win, as it jumped by 8.3% in the quarter, meaning that the company’s current strategy is working and offsetting the loss of volume. Regarding the automation gains, the hubs are set to be 28% more profitable than manual ones without accounting for the cost savings relating to labor. The company has cut nearly 100,000 jobs in over 2 years. Though I expect margins to continue to be slightly pressured in the first half of 2026, as the final 1 million packages per day of Amazon volume are shed and the costs of closing facilities hit the company’s financial books. I expect a hockey stick recovery in margins in the latter half, as we could possibly see net margin reaching back towards 10% and gross over 25%.

Risks

There is currently an empty space problem related to the Amazon glide-down. In the logistics world, profit depends on density, so filling trucks or planes to the maximum capacity. As UPS is shedding Amazon volume, it will have to close facilities and reduce staff fast enough to avoid paying for an oversized and underutilized network.

The 2023 Teamsters contract remains a double-edged sword, where the contract is front-loaded, meaning that wage and benefit costs are significantly higher in the first few years. Right now UPS is fighting against non-unionized rivals like Amazon Logistics that have much lower labor costs. If it can’t bridge the cap on those costs, it will be tougher to compete with them.

Conclusion

I believe that investing in UPS right now, as it is going through a massive transition with profitability in focus, is a great opportunity for investors. I believe that it is on track to have a great 2026, and thus I expect share prices to increase substantially enough to make it worthwhile for prospective investors, plus the +6% yield helps holding the company. I keep my Strong Buy rating on United Parcel Service.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.
